title: Abstract
content: Chemical processes that utilize CO 2 emissions from coal-fired power plants will be required as the world progresses towards reducing CO 2 emissions.The conversion of CO 2 using light energy (CO 2 photoreduction) has the potential to produce useful fuels or valuable chemicals while decreasing CO 2 emissions from the use of fossil fuels such as coal.Computational studies on the initial steps of photoinduced CO 2 activation on TiO 2 surfaces, necessary to develop a mechanistic understanding of CO 2 photoreduction are a focus of this article.The results from previous quantum mechanical modeling studies conducted by the authors indicated that stoichiometric TiO 2 surfaces likely do not promote electron transfer to CO 2 .Therefore, the role of oxygen vacancies in promoting the light-induced conversion of CO 2 (CO 2 photoreduction) on TiO 2 surfaces was examined in this study.Two different side-on bonded bent-CO 2 (bridging Ti-CO 2 --Ti species) were formed on the reduced rutile (110) and anatase ( 010), (001) surfaces, indicating charge transfer from the reduced surface to CO 2 .Further steps in the photoexcitation of these bent-CO 2 species were investigated with density functional theory calculations.Consistent with CO 2 adsorption and photodesorption on other n-type metal oxides such as ZrO 2 , the results suggest that the bent-CO 2 species do not gain further charge from the TiO 2 surface under illumination and are likely photodesorbed as neutral species.Additionally, although the formation of species such as CO and HCHO is thermodynamically possible, the energy needed to regenerate the oxygen vacancy on TiO 2 surfaces (7 eV) is greater than that available through band-gap illumination (3.2 eV).Therefore, CO 2 reactions with water on irradiated anatase TiO 2 surfaces are likely to be stoichiometric.

title: Experimental
content: All computations were performed in Turbomole V5.9 13.The B3LYP functional and the def2-SV(P) basis set were used.The calculations on small clusters with the def2-SV(P) basis set were performed without using the Resolution-of-Identity (RI) approximation.Basis set effects were tested by a B3LYPTZVPP calculation using the multipole-accelerated-resolution-of-identity-J (MARI-J) approximation and the TZVPP auxiliary basis set and a similar behavior was observed.Atomic charges for all clusters were calculated using natural population analysis (NPA) 14.All geometry optimizations were performed using default optimization criteria and the m3 grid. A neutral, non-stoichiometric, Ti 2 O 9 H 12 cluster (denoted as Ti2Vo110), based upon clusters used by de Lara-Castells and Krause 15 (non-stoichiometric, negatively charged Ti 2 O 9 H 11 -1 cluster) and Bredow 16 (non-stoichiometric, neutral Ti 2 O 9 H 12 cluster) was used to simulate the oxygen vacancy.Cerius 2 (MSI Inc.) was used to generate the (110) surface from the rutile bulk crystallographic data.Dangling bonds were saturated by adding hydrogen atoms at similar positions to Ti atoms in the bulk.All O-H bond distances were initially kept at 0.97 A.Only atoms on and adjacent to the (110) surface (four atoms in addition to CO 2 , the bridging oxygen atoms and the surface-Ti atoms) were optimized.Initially, neutral bare cluster geometries were studied.The singlet state of the oxygen-deficient cluster was the ground state.Subsequently, nine different configurations for CO 2 adsorption were modeled, and five unique configurations of CO 2 adsorption were obtained.The Ti 2 O 9 H 12 :CO 2 clusters are denoted as Ti2Vo110CO2(a-e).To compare the IR frequencies of bent-CO 2 species with experimental results, the bent-CO 2 clusters were fully relaxed, and the scaled (scale factor: 0.9614 17) harmonic vibrational frequencies were calculated.Previous studies by Ramis et al. 18, Rasko and Solymosi 3 and Papai et al. 19 were used to provide data for comparisons.Counterpoise-uncorrected CO 2 -TiO 2 cluster electronic interaction energies were calculated for these clusters using the following formula Eq. ( 1): A neutral, stoichiometric (010) Ti 2 O 8 H 10 cluster was used to model the oxygen vacancies on the anatase (010) surface.CO 2 adsorption on the oxygen vacancy was studied by varying the initial configurations of CO 2 .CO 2 adsorption on larger clusters, containing six Ti atoms, Ti 6 O 20 H 18 (denoted Ti6Vo010 and Ti6Vo001), was also modeled in this study.The IR frequencies and electronic interaction energies were calculated using procedures similar to those used for the rutile Ti2Vo110 clusters, described above. Frontier orbitals of various bent-CO 2 species were analyzed by plotting the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) using gOpenmol 20,21 at an isocontour value of 0.1 e 12 A 32 .Subsequently, geometry optimization was performed on selected clusters corresponding to bent-CO 2 species.

title: Results and discussion
content: Partially relaxed geometries of these CO 2 -reduced TiO 2 clusters (where only four atoms of the cluster and CO 2 were relaxed) are shown in Fig. 1(a-e).Only the titanium and the bridging twocoordinate (2-c) O atoms of the cluster were relaxed.The mode of binding affects whether charge transfer occurs to CO 2 .In Fig. 1(a-b), CO 2 primarily adsorbs as a linear species, in which the OCO bond angle is slightly more distorted in (b) compared to (a).Two bent-CO 2 species were modeled, shown in Fig. 1c andd. The notation for metal-CO 2 complexes used by Gibson 10 is used to differentiate between bent-CO 2 species Ti2Vo110CO2c-d.Accordingly, the Ti2Vo110CO2c species is assigned as a 2 - 4 species (because it has four bonds involving CO 2 and the two Ti atoms) and the Ti2Vo110CO2d is assigned as a 2 - 3 species (three bonds involving CO 2 and two Ti atoms).The Ti-Ti bond distance also increased slightly from 2.78 A in the case of the linearly bonded CO 2 species to 2.88-2.92A for the bent-CO 2 species.Additionally, although the OCO bond angle in Fig. 1d is smaller than that shown in Fig. 1c, the charge of the CO 2 -moiety is almost the same in both cases, as shown in Table 1. Interaction of CO 2 with the oxygen (3-c) atoms leads to the formation of the bicarbonate-like species shown in Fig. 1e.From the interaction energies presented in Table 2, it is clear that among the bent-CO 2 species, the 2 - 3 species is more stable than the 2 - 4 bent-CO 2 species.Both of these bent-CO 2 species are more stable than the linearly adsorbed CO 2 .The bicarbonate-like species, in turn, is more stable than the bent-CO 2 species. This non-exhaustive conformation search did not result in configurations that could lead to C-bonded CO 2 species, which represent 1 -C coordination between CO 2 and the metal center.However, Ramis et al. 18 and Rasko and Solymosi 3 both assigned bands in the IR spectrum to side-on bent and C-bonded CO 2 species upon CO 2 adsorption on TiO 2 .The most stable structures for CO 2 adsorption on the Co(110) surface from DFT studies 22 also correspond to structures somewhat similar to 2 - 3 and 2 - 4 configurations shown in Fig. 1.The "short-bridge" configuration on the Co(110) surface shown by de la Pena O'Shea et al. 22 is similar to the Ti2Vo110CO2c ( 2 - 4 ) species predicted in this study. Bent-CO 2 species formed on anatase surfaces are shown in Fig. 2. Similar to the rutile (110) surface, two kinds of bent-CO 2 species, ( 1 - 2 ) and ( 2 - 3 ), were observed on the anatase (010) surface.The ( 1 - 2 ) species (Ti6Vo010CO21 and Ti2Vo010CO22) converted to bridged ( 2 - 4 ) bent-CO 2 species similar to Ti2Vo110CO2c (from Fig. 1) upon relaxing all cluster atoms.Various configurations did not result in the formation of the C-bonded CO 2 species.The geometries of bent-CO 2 species in Fig. 2a (Ti6Vo010CO21 cluster) are somewhat similar to those in Fig. 2b (Ti2Vo010CO22 cluster). The OCO bond angles (130for the Ti6Vo010CO21 cluster and 133for Ti2Vo010CO22 cluster) are different, but the O-C bond lengths (1.30A and 1.20 A for the Ti6Vo010CO21 cluster, and 1.28 A and 1.21 A for the Ti2Vo010CO22 cluster, from Table 1) are comparable.This indicates that small two Ti atom clusters could be used in place of the larger, more computationally expensive Ti6-clusters.CO 2 forms a ( 2 - 3 ) complex on the anatase (001) surface, similar to the "long-bridge" configuration of de la Pena O'Shea et al. 22, but the bond angles and O-C bond lengths observed for the Ti6Vo001CO2 cluster in Fig. 2d are different from those calculated on the Co(110) surface.The O-C bond lengths are 1.25 and 1.29 A for the Ti6Vo001CO2 cluster, compared to 1.24 A for "longbridged" CO 2 species on the Co(110) surface.Similarly, the OCO bond angle for the Ti6Vo001CO2 cluster is significantly smaller compared to the 135bond angle of "long-bridged" CO 2 on the Co(110) surface. The calculated vibrational frequencies are shown in Fig. 3.The experimental data used for comparisons are shown in Table 3.The assignments of the side-on bonded and C-end bonded bent-CO 2 species by Ramis et al. 18, and Rasko and Solymosi 3 are inconsistent with earlier observations of CO 2 chemisorption on metal oxides by Busca and Lorenzelli 23.Busca and Lorenzelli attributed species exhibiting high values of 3 splittings (such as 1750, 1150 cm -1 ) and low thermal stabilities to "side-on" bent-CO 2 species exhibiting a 2 CO coordination, whereas Ramis et al. 18 assigned these frequencies to C-bonded bent-CO 2 species having a 1 C coordination to the Ti 3 electron centers.Our previous work 24 on CO 2 adsorption as carbonate on various anatase surface planes also revealed evidence for carbonate-like species with high 3 splittings.Therefore, high experimental 3 splittings measured experimentally might not always be indicative of negatively charged CO 2 -species. Papai et al. 19 calculated vibrational frequencies at 1800 cm -1 and 1000 cm -1 through DFT studies of 2 CO (side-on) CO 2 -species. Compared to 2 CO species, the 2 - 3 species would be bound more strongly to the surface, resulting in higher frequencies. Our modeling did not reveal C-bound CO 2 species having a 1 C coordination to the surface Ti 3 to be a stable species at 0 K. Therefore, the assignment of bent-CO 2 species to C-bound CO 2 species Figs. 3 and4, the larger (010) anatase Ti6-cluster gives results similar to the (010) anatase Ti2-cluster.Therefore, CO 2 adsorption on reduced TiO 2 surfaces is governed mostly by local interactions with the surface. Ground-state DFT calculations indicate that bent, negatively charged CO 2 species are formed upon CO 2 adsorption at surface two-coordinate oxygen vacancies on anatase and rutile TiO 2 surfaces.Subsequently, we studied further photoreactions of these species.The bent-CO 2 species could undergo thermal or photoreactions at the surface, forming CO, carbonates or desorbed CO 2 .Excited states of the chemisorbed CO 2 -species are likely to be multi-configurational. However, to simplify the analysis, we used frontier orbital analyses and TD-DFT excitation energies to analyze the potential for further charge transfer to CO 2 in the excited state. Frontier orbitals of the Ti6Vo010CO2 2 - 3 cluster are shown in Fig. 5.They indicate that CO 2 does not constitute the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) or (LUMO 1) orbitals of the Ti6Vo010CO2 complex.Therefore, electron transfer to CO 2 in the excited state is not possible.Moreover, because CO 2 contributes to the HOMO of the cluster, and given the fact that CO 2 does not contribute to the LUMO of the cluster strongly, light irradiation will likely result in the photodesorption of neutral CO 2 species from the reduced (010) surface. Emeline et al. 25 demonstrated that the adsorption of CO 2 on Zr 3 centers resulted in the formation of CO 2 -species.Upon irradiation, these species photodesorbed from the surface, resulting in the regeneration of Zr 3 centers.The frontier orbital analysis of the Ti6Vo010CO2 cluster is consistent with this result.CO 2 forms a negatively charged species upon adsorption at Ti 3 centers, formed due to the oxygen vacancies.Upon irradiation with light, these species are photooxidized and the resulting CO 2 desorbs from the surface.Similar photoreactions occur on n-type semiconductor surfaces as well (increasing the concentration of oxygen vacancies would make TiO 2 n-type).The orbital analysis presented in this study therefore does not support the findings of Rasko and Solymosi 3, where further electron transfer to the negatively charged CO 2 species occurred upon irradiation. CO formation via decomposition of the bent-CO 2 species, thus replenishing the oxygen vacancy, is energetically favorable.For example, the change in the electronic interaction energy for the reaction (Ti2Vo010CO21) Ti2010 CO (calculated at the B3LYPdef2-SV(P) level of theory) is -0.316 eV (-30 kJmol).Since CO 2 adsorption on the Ti2Vo010CO21 cluster is also energetically feasible, it follows that the formation of CO from gas phase CO 2 on reduced anatase (010) surfaces is feasible.Wilson et al. 26 found that CO 2 underwent C-C coupling reactions on reduced TiO 2 surfaces prepared by sputtering.However, this and similar reactions on other surfaces lead to the consumption of the oxygen vacancy. Possible photocatalytic cycles either involve a means to regenerate this oxygen vacancy or could involve a transformation of the bent-CO 2 species to a more electron-accepting species that undergoes further reactions under band-gap illumination.The energetics of such reactions involving CO 2 and H 2 O adsorbed on the Ti6Vo010 cluster were examined, and the results are summarized in Fig. 6.The results indicate that whereas C 1 species-forming reactions which consume oxygen vacancies are thermodynamically feasible, reactions which involve regeneration of the oxygen vacancies, thus completing the photocatalytic cycle are thermodynamically uphill, likely consuming more than one UV photon (because the band gap of TiO 2 (3.2 eV) is lower than the energy required to regenerate the surface oxygen vacancy (7 eV)).Moreover, as opposed to the original arguments for photoinduced hydrophilicity 27, experimental evidence in the literature suggests that interaction of low-intensity UVsolar radiation with TiO 2 surfaces is not likely to create oxygen vacancies.Experimental vibrational frequencies, cm -1 Ramis et al.Therefore, CO 2 photoreduction on reduced TiO 2 surfaces is likely to involve multiple photons or is a non-catalytic process, resulting in the consumption of the oxygen vacancy.

title: Conclusions
content: CO 2 adsorption and further reaction has been modeled using DFT calculations on the anatase (010), (001) and rutile (110) surfaces.CO 2 forms negatively charged, bent-CO 2 -species on surface oxygen vacancies on these surfaces.The bent-CO 2 species were more strongly held than the linear species (at 0 K).The "two-step desorption" of CO 2 observed during TDS studies on rutile (110) surfaces may be applicable to anatase (010), (001) surfaces as well.CO 2 adsorption is primarily local in nature.Two kinds of bent-CO 2 species were observed upon full relaxation of the cluster, having three ( 2 - 3 ) or four bonds ( 2 - 4 ) with the adjacent Ti atoms.The ( 2 - 3 ) species on rutile (110) surface are more stable than the ( 2 - 4 ) species.On all surfaces studied, the OCO bond angles of the ( 2 - 3 ) species are lower than those of the ( 2 - 4 ) species.CO 2 does not gain additional charge from the surface upon band-gap illumination, and likely desorbs as neutral CO 2 species.On the other hand, in the presence of coadsorbed water, the formation of C 1 species is thermodynamically feasible, resulting in the consumption of the oxygen vacancy.Re-creation of the oxygen vacancy is a key step in the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 on reduced TiO 2 surfaces.The data from the anatase (010) TiO 2 cluster indicate that the energy required to reform the oxygen vacancy on TiO 2 surfaces exceeds that of a band-gap energy-equivalent single UV photon.Therefore, photoreduction on reduced TiO 2 surfaces either is stoichiometric or consumes more than a single UV photon. Fig. 1.Partially relaxed geometries, of 2-Ti atom-CO 2 clusters (denoted as Ti2Vo110CO2(a-e)), from B3LYPdef2-SV(P) calculations.Configurations shown in (c-d) represent bent-CO 2 species.The bond distances are in A, the angles are in degrees. Fig. 2. Partially relaxed geometries of bent-CO 2 species from anatase surfaces from B3LYPdef2-SV(P) calculations.(a) (010) surface, 6 Ti atom cluster (Ti6Vo010CO2a), (b) (010) surface, 2 Ti atom cluster: (Ti2Vo010CO21), (c) (010) surface, 2 Ti atom cluster (Ti2Vo010CO22), (d): (001) surface, 6 Ti atom cluster (Ti6Vo001CO2).The bond distances are in A, the angles are in degrees. Fig. 4. C-O IR frequencies from the 2-Ti atom clusters from the rutile (110) surface (Ti2Vo110CO2c, Ti2Vo110CO2d) are consistent with those obtained from in situ FTIR spectroscopic studies of CO 2 adsorption on TiO 2 . Fig. 6.Relative energetics of reactions involving the formation of reduced C 1 species from gas phase CO 2 and water molecules on model cluster from the (010) anatase surface (Ti6Vo010).(A) Calculated from B3LYPdef2-SV(P) calculations: Gas phase CO 2 and H 2 O (located 7 A from the surface) of a triplet Ti6Vo010 cluster, (B) Singlet negatively charged CO 2 -species along with coadsorbed water molecule on a singlet Ti6Vo010 cluster, (C) Bicarbonate species coadsorbed with O surf H species, (D) Formate and hydroxyl groups on the surface, (E) Desorption of formaldehyde and the refilling of the oxygen vacancy, (F) Desorbed gas phase CO, H 2 O 2 and triplet state Ti6Vo010 cluster, (G) HCOOH and O desorbed from a triplet Ti6Vo010 cluster, (H) HCHO and O 2 desorbed from a triplet Ti6Vo010 cluster.Both singlet and triplet states were modeled, the state with the lowest energy is shown in the figure.The dotted arrows represent potential intermediates involved in the refilling of the oxygen vacancy.The energy required to desorb reduced C 1 species indicates that the reduction of CO 2 possibly consumes more than one photon. Fig.5.Frontier molecular orbitals of the ground-state singlet, neutral Ti6Vo010CO2 1 - 2 cluster, from B3LYPdef2-SV(P) calculations, shown at an isocontour value of 0.03 e 12 A 32 .CO 2 does not form acceptor states, as shown by the LUMO and LUMO 1 orbitals.Therefore, band-gap illumination of the surface would likely result in the desorption of bent-CO 2 as neutral species.(Plotted with the gOpenmol visualization program20,21). Type of bonding, C-O bond lengths, OCO bond angles and CO 2 -natural charges for bent-CO 2 species on anatase and rutile TiO 2 clusters.The results indicate that the structure of the surface plays a primary role in determining the amount of charge transferred to CO 2 . Counterpoise-uncorrected electronic interaction energies of various configurations of CO 2 adsorbed on rutile (110), obtained by B3LYPdef2-SV(P) calculations. is in question.Nevertheless, from Figs.3 and 4it is clear that the vibrational frequencies for CO 2 -species adsorbed on reduced rutile (110) surface are more consistent with the experimental values than corresponding values for CO 2 -species on anatase surfaces.From Ti6 2 - 3 : Ti6Vo001CO2.CO 2 adsorption on TiO 2 surfaces is structuresensitive.The experimental results are more consistent with the vibrational frequencies of bent-CO 2 species on the rutile (110) surface, compared to the anatase (001) and (010) surface. Experimental IR frequencies attributed to bent-CO 2 species on TiO 2 (from literature). V.P. Indrakanti et al. Fuel Processing Technology 92 (2011) 805-811 VPI thanks Penn State Institutes of Energy and the Environment (PSIEE) for financial support.Computational support was in part provided by the National Science Foundation under Grant No. CHE-0431328 for the Center for Environmental Kinetics Analysis (CEKA) at The Pennsylvania State University.
